name: Burpee's Golden
image_alt: Round golden yellow beet with orange flesh
description: A golden-yellow beet with orange flesh, sweet and mild with none of the earthiness of red beets. The great advantage: it doesn't bleed. Adds golden color to dishes without staining everything pink.
history: Introduced by W. Atlee Burpee in 1828 and continuously improved. The non-bleeding quality made it popular for cooking demonstrations and elegant dishes where red beet staining was undesirable.
seasonal_quirks: Germinates slightly slower than red beets — keep soil consistently moist. Flavor sweetens in cold conditions. Roots may be slightly smaller than red types at the same maturity stage.
harvest_cues: 2–3 inches diameter. The golden color is fully developed at maturity. Greens are edible but milder than red beet tops.
variety_notes: Use raw in salads where the golden color shows best — it fades to tan when roasted. Pairs beautifully with goat cheese and walnuts in classic presentations.
preservation: Roast and freeze. Pickle in apple cider vinegar for a beautiful golden pickle.
